title: Copyright and Training Data
concept: Generative models trained on large image datasets raise open legal and ethical questions about consent, attribution, and fair use of original artists' work.

exercises.prompt: What do generative AI models need to learn how to create realistic images?

options.text: Massive datasets of existing human-made images.
Only random numbers generated by a clock.
They do not need any training data at all.

exercises.explain: Generative AI models require millions of existing images, often created by humans, to learn how to generate new visuals.

cards.title: Whose Art is it Anyway?
cards.body: When you type a prompt and get a beautiful AI drawing, where did the AI learn to draw like that? It studied millions of real drawings made by human artists, often without their permission. Let's explore this digital tug-of-war!

cards.title: The Training Data Debate
cards.body: Generative AI models are trained on billions of images from the internet. Many of these images are copyrighted art, meaning they belong to the artists who made them. Using this data without permission raises big questions about whether it is fair or legal.

cards.title: The Copycat Chef
cards.body: Imagine a chef who eats at hundreds of local restaurants, tastes their secret recipes, and then opens a new bakery selling cakes that taste suspiciously similar to those local recipes—without ever asking the original chefs for permission or giving them credit. That's what generative AI does when it trains on artists' work.

cards.title: Style Mimicking in Action
cards.body: An illustrator notices that a popular AI image generator can perfectly mimic her unique, signature watercolor style. When she searches the dataset used to train the AI, she finds hundreds of her own online portfolio paintings were used to train it without her knowledge.

cards.title: Myth: AI Stores Real Images
cards.body: Many people think AI models store copies of original images inside their code. In reality, they don't store the images; they learn the patterns, styles, and shapes. However, they can still accidentally output images that look almost identical to the training data.

cards.title: Human vs. AI Learning
cards.body: Let's look at the difference between traditional art creation and how generative AI models learn from existing art.

left.text: Created from personal experience, imagination, and practice.

right.text: Generated by finding patterns in millions of scraped human artworks.

exercises.prompt: What is a major reason artists are filing lawsuits against AI companies?

options.text: The AI tools cost too much money to run.
AI images take too long to generate.
AI models are trained on artists' work without their permission.
AI models only use public domain images from the 1800s.

exercises.explain: Using copyrighted data to train AI without permission is a main driver of current lawsuits, as artists argue their creative rights are being violated.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The legal rules around AI training data and copyright are completely settled and agreed upon worldwide.
exercises.explain: AI training data ethics is still an active, unresolved debate in courts and communities worldwide.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the ethical concern.
exercises.sentence: Many artists argue that using their work without their _______ is unfair.
exercises.explain: Artists argue that AI companies should get explicit permission (consent) before using their creative work to train AI algorithms.

exercises.prompt: Which of these prompts raises the biggest ethical concern regarding artist style copying?

options.text: Create a photo of a generic red apple on a wooden table.
Draw a fantasy forest in the exact style of modern illustrator Sarah Land (by name).
Generate a blue circle on a black background.

exercises.explain: The prompt asking for a specific living artist's unique style by name is highly likely to rely on unauthorized training data of that artist's work.

exercises.prompt: Why is training AI models on online art datasets a highly controversial topic today?

options.text: It is automatically legal and fully supported by all artists.
It makes the AI model forget its original programming.
It creates major legal and ethical debates around consent and fair use.
It guarantees that the AI will only make identical copies of images.

exercises.explain: Using copyrighted work to train AI models without permission raises massive ethical and legal debates about consent, fair use, and how creators should be credited and compensated.
